subject: Chemistry
question_text: Haloalkanes react with KCN to form alkyl cyanides as the main product while AgCN forms isocyanides as the chief product. Explain.
question_html: Haloalkanes react with KCN to form alkyl cyanides as the main product while AgCN forms isocyanides as the chief product. Explain.
solution_text: KCN form alkyl cyanide as a major products as it is predominantly ionic and provides cyanide ions in solution. Although both carbon and nitrogen atoms of CN are in a position to donate electron pairs, the attack takes place mainly through carbon atom and not through nitrogen atom since C—C bond is more stable than C—N bond. However, AgCN is mainly covalent in nature and nitrogen is free to donate electron pair forming isocyanide as the main product.

question_text: Predict the order of reactivity of the following compounds in S N 1 and S N 2 reactions: The four isomeric bromobutanes C 6 H 5 CH 2 Br, C 6 H 5 CH(C 6 H 5 )Br, C 6 H 5 CH(CH 3 )Br, C 6 H 5 C(CH 3 )(C 6 H 5 )Br
question_html:
Predict the order of reactivity of the following compounds in SN1 and SN2 reactions:

  1. The four isomeric bromobutanes
  2. C6H5CH2Br, C6H5CH(C6H5)Br, C6H5CH(CH3)Br, C6H5C(CH3)(C6H5)Br
solution_text: CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 Br < (CH 3 ) 2 CHCH 2 Br < CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)CH 3 < (CH 3 ) 3 CBr (S N 1) CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 Br > (CH 3 ) 2 CHCH 2 Br > CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)CH 3 > (CH 3 ) 3 CBr (S N 2) Of the two primary bromides, the carbocation intermediate derived from (CH 3 ) 2 CHCH 2 Br is more stable than derived from CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 Br because of greater electron-donating inductive effect of (CH 3 ) 2 CH- group. Therefore, (CH 3 ) 2 CHCH 2 Br is more reactive than CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 Br in S N 1 (unimolecular substitution) reactions. CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)CH 3 is a secondary bromide and (CH 3 ) 3 CBr is a tertiary bromide. Hence the above order is followed in S N 1 unimolecular substitution reaction. The reactivity in S N 2 (bimolecular substitution) reactions follows the reverse order as the steric hindrance around the electrophilic carbon increases in that order. C 6 H 5 C(CH 3 )(C 6 H 5 )Br > C 6 H 5 CH(C 6 H 5 )Br > C 6 H 5 CH(CH 3 )Br > C 6 H 5 CH 2 Br (S N 1) C 6 H 5 C(CH 3 )(C 6 H 5 )Br < C 6 H 5 CH(C 6 H 5 )Br < C 6 H 5 CH(CH 3 )Br < C 6 H 5 CH 2 Br (SN 2 ) Of the two secondary bromides, the carbocation intermediate obtained from C 6 H 5 CH(C 6 H 5 )Br is more stable than obtained from C 6 H 5 CH(CH 3 )Br because it is stabilized by two phenyl groups due to resonance. Therefore, the former bromide is more reactive than the latter in S N 1unimolecular substitution reactions. A phenyl group is bulkier than a methyl group. Therefore, C 6 H 5 CH(C 6 H 5 )Br is less reactive than C 6 H 5 CH(CH 3 )Br in S N 2 bimolecular substitution reactions.
solution_html:
1. CH3CH2CH2CH2Br < (CH3)2CHCH2Br < CH3CH2CH(Br)CH3 < (CH3)3CBr (SN1) CH3CH2CH2CH2Br > (CH3)2CHCH2Br > CH3CH2CH(Br)CH3 > (CH3)3CBr (SN2)
    Of the two primary bromides, the carbocation intermediate derived from (CH3)2CHCH2Br is more stable than derived from CH3CH2CH2CH2Br because of greater electron-donating inductive effect of (CH3)2CH- group. Therefore, (CH3)2CHCH2Br is more reactive than CH3CH2CH2CH2Br in SN1 (unimolecular substitution) reactions. CH3CH2CH(Br)CH3 is a secondary bromide and (CH3)3CBr is a tertiary bromide. Hence the above order is followed in SN1 unimolecular substitution reaction. The reactivity in SN2 (bimolecular substitution) reactions follows the reverse order as the steric hindrance around the electrophilic carbon increases in that order.
  2. C6H5C(CH3)(C6H5)Br > C6H5CH(C6H5)Br > C6H5CH(CH3)Br > C6H5CH2Br (SN1) C6H5C(CH3)(C6H5)Br < C6H5CH(C6H5)Br < C6H5CH(CH3)Br < C6H5CH2Br (SN2)
    Of the two secondary bromides, the carbocation intermediate obtained from C6H5CH(C6H5)Br is more stable than obtained from C6H5CH(CH3)Br because it is stabilized by two phenyl groups due to resonance. Therefore, the former bromide is more reactive than the latter in SN1unimolecular substitution reactions. A phenyl group is bulkier than a methyl group. Therefore, C6H5CH(C6H5)Br is less reactive than C6H5CH(CH3)Br in SN2 bimolecular substitution reactions.

question_text: Although chlorine is an electron withdrawing group, yet it is ortho-, para- directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Why?
question_html: Although chlorine is an electron withdrawing group, yet it is ortho-, para- directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Why?
solution_text: Chlorine is electron withdrawing in nature but due to the strong +R effect, one of the lone pair on chlorine atom conjugates with the benzene ring in chlorobenzene. So, it is the resonance hybrid of the following structures: It is clear from resonating structures of chlorobenzene, the electron density is maximum at o- and p-positions due to +R- effect. Therefore an electrophile (E + ) can easily attack at such positions. Cl donates its lone pair of electrons to the aromatic ring. Hence, chlorobenzene is o,p-directing towards electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Why is sulphuric acid not used during the reaction of alcohols with KI?
question_html: Why is sulphuric acid not used during the reaction of alcohols with KI?
solution_text: In the presence of sulphuric acid (H 2 SO 4 ),KI produces HI 2KI + H 2 SO 4 {tex}\to{/tex} 2KHSO 4 + 2HI Since H 2 SO 4 is an oxidizing agent, it oxidizes HI (produced in the reaction to I 2 ). 2HI + H 2 SO 4 {tex}\to{/tex} I 2 + SO 2 + H 2 O As a result, the reaction between alcohol and HI to produce alkyl iodide cannot occur. Therefore, sulphuric acid is not used during the reaction of alcohols with KI. Instead, a non-oxidizing acid such as H 3 PO 4 is used.
solution_html:
In the presence of sulphuric acid (H2SO4),KI produces HI
2KI + H2SO4 {tex}\to{/tex} 2KHSO4 + 2HI
Since H2SO4 is an oxidizing agent, it oxidizes HI (produced in the reaction to I2).
2HI + H2SO4 {tex}\to{/tex} I2 + SO2 + H2O
As a result, the reaction between alcohol and HI to produce alkyl iodide cannot occur. Therefore, sulphuric acid is not used during the reaction of alcohols with KI. Instead, a non-oxidizing acid such as H3PO4 is used.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Among the isomeric alkanes of molecular formula C 5 H 12 , identify the one that on photochemical chlorination yields A single monochloride. Three isomeric monochlorides. Four isomeric monochlorides.
question_html:
Among the isomeric alkanes of molecular formula C5H12, identify the one that on photochemical chlorination yields

  1. A single monochloride.
  2. Three isomeric monochlorides.
  3. Four isomeric monochlorides.
solution_text: To have a single monochloride, there should be only one type of H-atom in the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C 5 H 12 .This is because, replacement of any H-atom leads to the formation of the same product. The isomer is neopentane. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}{/tex} To have three isomeric monochlorides, the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C 5 H 12 should contain three different types of H-atoms. Therefore, the isomer is n-pentane. It can be observed that there are three types of H atoms labelled as a, b and c in n-pentane. {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^c - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^b - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^a - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^b - \mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^c }\limits_{n - {\text{Pentane}}}{/tex} To have four isomeric monochlorides, the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C 5 H 12 should contain four different types of H-atoms. Therefore, the isomer is 2-methylbutane. It can be observed that there are four types of H-atoms labelled as a, b, c, and d in 2-methylbutane. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^a - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^b }\limits^| }\limits^{\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^a } - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^c - \mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^d{/tex}
solution_html:
1. To have a single monochloride, there should be only one type of H-atom in the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C5H12.This is because, replacement of any H-atom leads to the formation of the same product. The isomer is neopentane.
    {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}{/tex}
  2. To have three isomeric monochlorides, the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C5H12 should contain three different types of H-atoms. Therefore, the isomer is n-pentane. It can be observed that there are three types of H atoms labelled as a, b and c in n-pentane.
    {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^c - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^b - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^a - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^b - \mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^c }\limits_{n - {\text{Pentane}}}{/tex}
  3. To have four isomeric monochlorides, the isomer of the alkane of the molecular formula C5H12 should contain four different types of H-atoms. Therefore, the isomer is 2-methylbutane. It can be observed that there are four types of H-atoms labelled as a, b, c, and d in 2-methylbutane.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^a - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^b }\limits^| }\limits^{\mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^a } - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^c - \mathop {C{H_3}}\limits^d{/tex}

question_text: Arrange each set of compounds in order of increasing boiling points. Bromomethane, Bromoform, Chloromethane, Dibromomethane. 1-Chloropropane, Isopropyl chloride, 1-Chlorobutane.
question_html:
Arrange each set of compounds in order of increasing boiling points.

  1. Bromomethane, Bromoform, Chloromethane, Dibromomethane.
  2. 1-Chloropropane, Isopropyl chloride, 1-Chlorobutane.
solution_text: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - Cl}\limits_{Chloromethane}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - Br}\limits_{Bromomethane}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_2}}\limits^| }\limits^{Br} - Br}\limits_{Dibromomethane}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {Br - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{Br} - Br}\limits_{Bromoform}{/tex} For alkyl halides containing the same alkyl group, the boiling point increases with an increase in the atomic mass of the halogen atom as the vander waal forces increase with increase in mass of the molecules. Since the atomic mass of Br is greater than that of Cl, the boiling point of bromomethane is higher than that of chloromethane. Further, for alkyl halides containing the same alkyl group, the boiling point increases with an increase in the number of halides. Therefore, the boiling point of dibromomethane is higher than that of chloromethane and bromomethane, but lower than that of bromoform. Hence, the given set of compounds can be arranged in the order of their increasing boiling points as: Chloromethane < Bromomethane < Dibromomethane < Bromoform. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{Cl} - C{H_3}}\limits_{Isopropyl\;chloride}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {Cl - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{1 - Chloropropane}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {Cl - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{1 - {\text{Chlorobutane}}}{/tex} For alkyl halides containing the same halide, the boiling point increases with an increase in the size of the alkyl group. Thus, the boiling point of 1-chlorobutane is higher than that of isopropyl chloride and 1-chloropropane. Further, the boiling point decreases with an increase in branching in the chain as the points of interaction between the molecules decrease with increase in branching. Thus, the boiling point of isopropyl alcohol is lower than that of 1-chloropropane. Hence, the given set of compounds can be arranged in the increasing order of their boiling points as: Isopropyl chloride < 1-Chloropropane < 1-Chlorobutane

Since the atomic mass of Br is greater than that of Cl, the boiling point of bromomethane is higher than that of chloromethane. Further, the boiling point decreases with an increase in branching in the chain as the points of interaction between the molecules decrease with increase in branching. Thus, the boiling point of isopropyl alcohol is lower than that of 1-chloropropane. Therefore, 1-bromobutane reacts more rapidly than 2-bromobutane by an S N 2 mechanism. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits_| }\limits_{Br} - C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - {\text{Bromobutane}}(2^\circ )}, \;\;\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br}\limits_{2 - Bromo - 2 - methylpropane(3^\circ )}{/tex} 2-Bromobutane is {tex}2^\circ {/tex} alkylhalide whereas 2-bromo-2-methylpropane is {tex}3^\circ {/tex} alkyl halide. Therefore, greater numbers of substituents are present in {tex}3^\circ {/tex} alkyl halide than in {tex}2^\circ {/tex} alkyl halide to hinder the approaching nucleophile. Hence, 2-bromobutane reacts more rapidly than 2-bromo-2-methylpropane by an {tex}{S_N}2{/tex} mechanism. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits_| }\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - Br}\limits_{1 - Bromo - 3 - {\text{methylbutane(1}}^\circ {\text{)}}}{/tex} , {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits_| }\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - Br}\limits_{1 - Bromo - 2 - {\text{methylbutane}}(1^\circ )}{/tex} Both the alkyl halides are primary. However, the substituent {tex}- C{H_3}{/tex} is at a greater distance from the carbon atom linked to -Br in 1-bromo-3-methylbutane than in 1-bromo-2-methylbutane. Therefore, the approaching nucleophile is less hindered in case of the former than in case of the latter. Hence, the former reacts faster than the latter by {tex}{S_N}2{/tex} mechanism.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Identify A, B, C, D, E, R and {tex}{R^1}{/tex} in the following: + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{{dry\,ether}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{H_2O}}{/tex} B R - Br + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{{dry\,ether}}{/tex} C {tex}\xrightarrow{{d_2O}} C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_D HC{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\xleftarrow{{Na/ether}}{/tex} R 1 - X {tex}\xrightarrow{{Mg}}{/tex} D {tex}\xrightarrow{{H_2O}}{/tex} E
question_html:
Identify A, B, C, D, E, R and {tex}{R^1}{/tex} in the following:
+ Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{{dry\,ether}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{H_2O}}{/tex} B
R - Br + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{{dry\,ether}}{/tex} C {tex}\xrightarrow{{d_2O}} C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_D HC{H_3}{/tex}
{tex}\xleftarrow{{Na/ether}}{/tex}R1 - X {tex}\xrightarrow{{Mg}}{/tex} D {tex}\xrightarrow{{H_2O}}{/tex} E
solution_text: An alkyl halide on treatment with Mg in dry ether gives the Grignard reagent. So A is cyclohexyl magnesium bromide as shown which on treatment with water gives the alkane i.e. cyclohexane (B) Since D of {tex}{D_2}O{/tex} gets attached to the carbon atom to which MgBr is attached, C is {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CHC{H_3}}\limits_| }\limits_{MgBr} }\limits_{{\text{Isopropyl magnesium bromide}}} {/tex} Therefore, compound D formed on reaction of the halide with Mg is {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - MgBr}\limits_{tert - Butyl magnesiumbromide}{/tex} Therefore, the compound R - Br is {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CHC{H_3}}\limits_| }\limits_{Br} }\limits_{{\text{Isopropyl bromide}}} {/tex} When an alkyl halide is treated with Na in the presence of ether, a hydrocarbon containing double the number of carbon atoms as present in the original halide is obtained as product. This is known as Wurtz reaction. Therefore, the halide, R 1 - X, is {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - X}\limits_{tert - Butylhalide}{/tex} And, compound E formed on reaction of tert-butyl magnesium bromide is {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} -H}\limits_{2-methylpropane}{/tex}
solution_html:
An alkyl halide on treatment with Mg in dry ether gives the Grignard reagent. So A is cyclohexyl magnesium bromide as shown which on treatment with water gives the alkane i.e. cyclohexane (B)
Since D of {tex}{D_2}O{/tex} gets attached to the carbon atom to which MgBr is attached, C is
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CHC{H_3}}\limits_| }\limits_{MgBr} }\limits_{{\text{Isopropyl magnesium bromide}}} {/tex}
Therefore, compound D formed on reaction of the halide with Mg is
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - MgBr}\limits_{tert - Butyl magnesiumbromide}{/tex}
Therefore, the compound R - Br is

{tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CHC{H_3}}\limits_| }\limits_{Br} }\limits_{{\text{Isopropyl bromide}}} {/tex}
When an alkyl halide is treated with Na in the presence of ether, a hydrocarbon containing double the number of carbon atoms as present in the original halide is obtained as product. This is known as Wurtz reaction. Therefore, the halide, R1 - X, is
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - X}\limits_{tert - Butylhalide}{/tex}
And, compound E formed on reaction of tert-butyl magnesium bromide is
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} -H}\limits_{2-methylpropane}{/tex}

solution_text: Dichlormethane (CH 2 Cl 2 ) {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.60D Chloroform (CHCl 3 ) {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.08D Carbon tetrachloride (​​​​​​​CCl 4 ) {tex}\mu{/tex} = 0D ​​​​​​​CCl 4 is a symmetrical molecule. Therefore, the dipole moments of all four C-Cl bonds cancel each other. Hence, its resultant dipole moment is zero. As shown in the above figure, in ​​​​​​​CHCl 3 ,the resultant of dipole moments of two C-Cl bonds is opposed by the resultant of dipole moments of one C-H bond and one C-Cl bond. Since the resultant of one C-H bond and one C-Cl bond dipole moments is smaller than two C-Cl bonds, the opposition is to a small extent. As a result, has a smaller dipole moment of 1.08 D. On the other hand, in case of CH 2 Cl 2 , the resultant of the dipole moments of two C-Cl bonds is strengthened by the resultant of the dipole moments of two C-H bonds. As a result, ​​​​​​​CH 2 Cl 2 has a higher dipole moment of 1.60 D than ​​​​​​​CHCl 3 i.e., ​​​​​​​CH 2 Cl 2 has the highest dipole moment. Hence, the given compounds can be arranged in the increasing order of their dipole moments as: CCl 4 < CHCl 3 < CH 2 Cl 2

question_text: A hydrocarbon C 5 H 10 does not react with chlorine in dark but gives a single monochloro compound ​​​​​​​C 5 H 9 Cl in bright sunlight. Identify the hydrocarbon.
question_html: A hydrocarbon C5H10 does not react with chlorine in dark but gives a single monochloro compound ​​​​​​​C5H9Cl in bright sunlight. Identify the hydrocarbon.
solution_text: A hydrocarbon with the molecular formula, C 5 H 10 belongs to the group with a general molecular formula ​​​​​​​C n H 2n .Therefore, it may either be an alkene or a cycloalkane. Since hydrocarbon does not react with chlorine in the dark, it cannot be an alkene. Thus, it should be a cycloalkane. Further, the hydrocarbon gives a single monochloro compound, ​​​​​​​C 5 H 9 Cl by reacting with chlorine in bright sunlight. Since a single monochloro compound is formed, the hydrocarbon must contain H-atoms that are all equivalent. Also, as all H-atoms of a cycloalkane are equivalent, the hydrocarbon must be a cycloalkane. Hence, the said compound is cyclopentane. question_text: What are ambident nucleophiles? Explain with an example.
question_html: What are ambident nucleophiles? Explain with an example.
solution_text: Ambident nucleophiles are nucleophiles having two nucleophilic sites. Thus, ambident nucleophiles have two sites through which they can attack. For example, nitrite ion is an ambident nucleophile. {tex}\left[ {{}^ - O - \ddot N = O} \right]{/tex} Nitrite ion can attack through oxygen resulting in the formation of alkyl nitrites. Also, it can attack through nitrogen resulting in the formation of nitroalkanes.

question_text: Which compound in each of the following pairs will react faster in S N 2 reaction with OH - ? CH 3 Br or CH 3 I (CH 3 ) 3 CCl or CH 3 Cl
question_html:
Which compound in each of the following pairs will react faster in SN2 reaction with OH-?

  1. CH3Br or CH3I
  2. (CH3)3 CCl or CH3Cl
solution_text: Since I - ion is a better leaving group than Br - ion, therefore, CH 3 I will react faster than CH 3 Br in S N 2 reaction with OH - ion. On steric grounds 1° alkyl halides are more reactive than tert-alkyl halide in S N 2 reactions. Since this reaction requires the approach of the nucleophile to the carbon bearing the leaving group, the presence of bulky substituents on or near the carbon atom have a dramatic inhibiting effect. Therefore, CH 3 Cl will react at a faster rate than (CH 3 ) 3 CCl in a S N 2 reaction with OH - ion.

1. Since I- ion is a better leaving group than Br- ion, therefore, CH3I will react faster than CH3Br in SN2 reaction with OH-ion.

Since this reaction requires the approach of the nucleophile to the carbon bearing the leaving group, the presence of bulky substituents on or near the carbon atom have a dramatic inhibiting effect. Therefore, CH3Cl will react at a faster rate than  (CH3)3 CCl in a SN2 reaction with OH-ion.

question_text: Explain why the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride? alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water? Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
question_html:
Explain why

  1. the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride?
  2. alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water?
  3. Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
solution_text: In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 2 hybridized carbon atom. In cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 3 hybridized carbon atom. Now, sp 2 hybridized carbon has more s-character than sp 3 hybridized carbon atom. Therefore, the former is more electronegative than the latter. Therefore, the density of electrons of C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride. Moreover, the - R effect of the benzene ring of chlorobenzene decreases the electron density of the C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom. As a result, the polarity of the C - Cl bond in chlorobenzene decreases. Hence, the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride. To be miscible with water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water forces of attraction. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with water. Grignard reagents are very reactive. In the presence of moisture, they react to give alkanes. {tex}\mathop {\mathop R\limits^{\delta - } \mathop M\limits^{\delta + } g\mathop X\limits^{\delta - } }\limits_{Grigrard\;reagent} + {H_2}O \to \mathop {R - H}\limits_{Alkane} + Mg(OH)X{/tex} Therefore, Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions.
solution_html:
1. In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp2 hybridized carbon atom. In cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp3 hybridized carbon atom. Now, sp2 hybridized carbon has more s-character than sp3 hybridized carbon atom. Therefore, the former is more electronegative than the latter. Therefore, the density of electrons of C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride. Moreover, the - R effect of the benzene ring of chlorobenzene decreases the electron density of the C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom. As a result, the polarity of the C - Cl bond in chlorobenzene decreases. Hence, the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride.
  2. To be miscible with water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water forces of attraction. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with water.
  3. Grignard reagents are very reactive. In the presence of moisture, they react to give alkanes.
    {tex}\mathop {\mathop R\limits^{\delta - } \mathop M\limits^{\delta + } g\mathop X\limits^{\delta - } }\limits_{Grigrard\;reagent} + {H_2}O \to \mathop {R - H}\limits_{Alkane} + Mg(OH)X{/tex}
    Therefore, Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give the uses of freon 12, DDT, carbon tetrachloride and iodoform.
question_html: Give the uses of freon 12, DDT, carbon tetrachloride and iodoform.
solution_text: Uses of Freon - 12: Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane, {tex}C{F_2}C{l_2}{/tex} is commonly known as CFC. It is used as a refrigerant in refrigerators and air conditioners. It is also used in aerosol spray propellants such as body sprays, hair sprays, etc. However, it damages the ozone layer. Uses of DDT: DDT (p, p-dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) is one of the best known insecticides. It is very effective against mosquitoes and lice. Uses of carbontetrachloride {tex}CC{l_4}{/tex} : It is used for manufacturing refrigerants and propellants for aerosol cans. It is used as feedstock in the synthesis of chlorofluorocarbons and other chemicals. It is used as a solvent in the manufacture of pharmaceutical products. Until the mid 1960's, carbon tetrachloride was widely used as a cleaning fluid, a degreasing agent in industries, a spot reamer in homes, and a fire extinguisher. Uses of iodoform {tex}\left( {{\mathbf{CH}}{{\mathbf{I}}_{\mathbf{3}}}} \right){/tex} : Iodoform was used earlier as an antiseptic, but now it has been replaced by other formulations-containing iodine-due to its objectionable smell. The antiseptic property of iodoform is only due to the liberation of free iodine when it comes in contact with the skin.
solution_html:
Uses of Freon - 12: Freon-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane, {tex}C{F_2}C{l_2}{/tex} is commonly known as CFC. It is used as a refrigerant in refrigerators and air conditioners. It is also used in aerosol spray propellants such as body sprays, hair sprays, etc. However, it damages the ozone layer.

Uses of DDT: DDT (p, p-dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) is one of the best known insecticides. It is very effective against mosquitoes and lice.

Uses of carbontetrachloride {tex}CC{l_4}{/tex}:

  1. It is used for manufacturing refrigerants and propellants for aerosol cans.
  2. It is used as feedstock in the synthesis of chlorofluorocarbons and other chemicals.
  3. It is used as a solvent in the manufacture of pharmaceutical products.
  4. Until the mid 1960's, carbon tetrachloride was widely used as a cleaning fluid, a degreasing agent in industries, a spot reamer in homes, and a fire extinguisher.

Uses of iodoform {tex}\left( {{\mathbf{CH}}{{\mathbf{I}}_{\mathbf{3}}}} \right){/tex}: Iodoform was used earlier as an antiseptic, but now it has been replaced by other formulations-containing iodine-due to its objectionable smell. The antiseptic property of iodoform is only due to the liberation of free iodine when it comes in contact with the skin.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the mechanism of the following reaction: {tex}n-BuBr + KCN\xrightarrow{{EtOH - {H_2}O}}nBuCN{/tex}
question_html:
Write the mechanism of the following reaction:
{tex}n-BuBr + KCN\xrightarrow{{EtOH - {H_2}O}}nBuCN{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}n-BuBr + KCN\xrightarrow{{EtOH - {H_2}O}}n-BuCN{/tex} The given reaction is a nucleophilic substitution reaction. In this reaction, CN - acts as the nucleophile and attacks the carbon atom to which Br is attached. CN - ion is an ambident nucleophile and can attack through both C and N. In this case, it attacks through the C-atom.

question_text: Arrange the compounds of each set in order of reactivity towards S N 2 displacement: 2-bromo-2-methyl butane,1-bromopentane, 2-bromopentane. 1-bromo-3-methylbutane, 2-bromo-2-methylbutane, 3-bromo-2-methylbutane. 1-bromobutane, 1-bromo-2, 2-dimethylpropane, 1-bromo-2-methylbutane, 1-bromo-3-methylbutane.
question_html:
Arrange the compounds of each set in order of reactivity towards SN2 displacement:

  1. 2-bromo-2-methyl butane,1-bromopentane, 2-bromopentane.
  2. 1-bromo-3-methylbutane, 2-bromo-2-methylbutane, 3-bromo-2-methylbutane.
  3. 1-bromobutane, 1-bromo-2, 2-dimethylpropane, 1-bromo-2-methylbutane, 1-bromo-3-methylbutane.
solution_text: In S N 2 reaction, steric factors determine the reactivity. more reactive alkyl halides have less steric hindrance. Hence, the decreasing order of the reactivity of alkyl halides is 1 o > 2 o > 3 o . The order of reactivity as follows: 1-bromopentane > 2-bromopentane > 2-bromo-2-methylbutane 1-bromo-3-methylbutane > 3-bromo-2-methylbutane > 2-bromo-2-methylbutane 1-bromobutane > 1-bromo-3-methylbutane > 1-bromo-2-methylbutane> 1-bromo-2, 2 dimethylpropane

In SN2 reaction, steric factors determine the reactivity. more reactive alkyl halides have less steric hindrance. Hence, the decreasing order of the reactivity of alkyl halides is 1o > 2o > 3o. The order of reactivity as follows:

  1. 1-bromopentane > 2-bromopentane > 2-bromo-2-methylbutane
  2. 1-bromo-3-methylbutane > 3-bromo-2-methylbutane > 2-bromo-2-methylbutane

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Out of C 6 H 5 CH 2 Cl, and C 6 H 5 CHClC 6 H 5 , which is more easily hydrolysed by aqueous KOH?
question_html: Out of C6H5CH2Cl, and C6H5CHClC6H5, which is more easily hydrolysed by aqueous KOH?
solution_text: Hydrolysis by aqueous KOH proceeds through the formation of carbocation. If carbocation is stable, then the compound is easily hydrolyzed by aqueous KOH. Now C 6 H 5 CH 2 Cl forms {tex}1^\circ - {/tex} carbocation, while C 6 H 5 CHClC 6 H 5 forms {tex}2^\circ -{/tex} carbocation, which is more stable than {tex}1^\circ - {/tex} carbocation. Hence, C 6 H 5 CHClC 6 H 5 is hydrolyzed more easily than C 6 H 5 CH 2 Cl by aqueous KOH.

Now C6H5CH2Cl forms {tex}1^\circ - {/tex} carbocation, while C6H5CHClC6H5 forms {tex}2^\circ -{/tex} carbocation, which is more stable than {tex}1^\circ - {/tex} carbocation. Hence, C6H5CHClC6H5 is hydrolyzed more easily than C6H5CH2Cl by aqueous KOH.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: p-Dichlorobenzene has higher m.p. than those of o- and m-isomers. Discuss.
question_html: p-Dichlorobenzene has higher m.p. than those of o- and m-isomers. Discuss.
solution_text: p-Dichlorobenzene is more symmetrical than o-and m-isomers. For this reason, it fits more closely than o-and m-isomers in the crystal lattice. Therefore, more energy is required to break the crystal lattice of p-dichlorobenzene. As a result, p-dichlorobenzene has a higher melting point and lower solubility than o-and m-isomers.

  11. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}Cl}\limits_{Ethyl\,\,chloride} \xrightarrow[{(\mathop {Nucleophilic}\limits_{substitution)} }]{{KCN,EtOH - {H_2}O}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CN}\limits_{\Pr opanentrite}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Hydrolysis}]{{{H^ + }/{H_2}O}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{\Pr opanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}
  12. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{But - 1 - ene} \xrightarrow[{Anti - Mark\,\,Addition}]{{HBr/RCOOR}}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Finkelstein\,reaction}]{{Nal.acetone}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}I}\limits_{n - Butyliodide} {/tex}
  13. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Cl} } H - C{H_3}}\limits_{But - 1 - ene} \xrightarrow[{Dehydroha\log enation}]{{KOH(alc),\Delta }}\mathop {C{H_3} - CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{\Pr opene} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Anti - Mark\,addition}]{{HBr,\operatorname{P} eroxide}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br}\limits_{1 - Bromo - propane} \xrightarrow{{KOH(aq),\Delta }}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH{/tex}
  14. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_3} + 4{I_2} + 6NaOH\xrightarrow[{Iodoform\,\,reaction}]{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}\mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{Iodoform} + C{H_3}COONa + 5NaI + 5{H_2}O{/tex}
  16. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Br} } H - C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - Bromoprpane} \xrightarrow[{Dehydroha\log enation}]{{KOH(alc),\Delta }}C{H_3}CH = C{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Peroxide\,\,effect}]{{HBr/Peroxide}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br}\limits_{1 - Bronopropane}{/tex}
  17. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}C{H_2} - Cl}\limits_{Chloroethane} + 2Na\xrightarrow[{Wurtz\,\,reaction}]{{Dry\,\,ether}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2} - C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{Bu\tan e} + 2NaCl{/tex}
  19. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Br} }^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}}\limits_{tert - Butylbromide} \xrightarrow[{Dehydroha\log enation}]{{KOH(alc),\Delta }}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } = C{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Anti\,\,Mark.\,Addition}]{{HBr,Peroxide}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2}Br}\limits_{Isobutyl\,\,bromide} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The treatment of alkyl chlorides with aqueous KOH leads to the formation of alcohols but in the presence of alcoholic KOH, alkenes are major products. Explain.
question_html: The treatment of alkyl chlorides with aqueous KOH leads to the formation of alcohols but in the presence of alcoholic KOH, alkenes are major products. Explain.
solution_text: In an aqueous solution, KOH almost completely ionizes to give OH - ions. OH - ion is a strong nucleophile, which leads the alkyl chloride to undergo a substitution reaction to form alcohol. {tex}\mathop {\mathop {R - Cl + KO{H_{(aq)}}}\limits_{Alkyl} }\limits_{chloride} \to \mathop {R - OH + KCl}\limits_{Alcohol}{/tex} On the other hand, an alcoholic solution of KOH contains alkoxide {tex}\left( {R{O^\;}^{ - \;}} \right){/tex} ion, which is a strong base. Thus, it can abstract a hydrogen from the $\beta $ carbon of the alkyl chloride and form an alkene by eliminating a molecule of HCl. {tex}\mathop {R - \mathop C\limits_\beta {H_2} - \mathop C\limits_\alpha {H_2}}\limits_{Alkyl\;chloride} - Cl + KOH(alc) \to{/tex} {tex}\mathop {R - CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{Alkene} + KCl + {H_2}O{/tex} {tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion is a much weaker base than {tex} RO^ -{/tex} ion. Also, {tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion is highly solvated in an aqueous solution and as a result, the basic character of {tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion decreases. Therefore, it cannot abstract a hydrogen from the {tex}\beta {/tex} -carbon. {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
In an aqueous solution, KOH almost completely ionizes to give OH - ions. OH - ion is a strong nucleophile, which leads the alkyl chloride to undergo a substitution reaction to form alcohol.
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {R - Cl + KO{H_{(aq)}}}\limits_{Alkyl} }\limits_{chloride} \to \mathop {R - OH + KCl}\limits_{Alcohol}{/tex}
On the other hand, an alcoholic solution of KOH contains alkoxide {tex}\left( {R{O^\;}^{ - \;}} \right){/tex}ion, which is a strong base. Thus, it can abstract a hydrogen from the $\beta $ carbon of the alkyl chloride and form an alkene by eliminating a molecule of HCl.
{tex}\mathop {R - \mathop C\limits_\beta {H_2} - \mathop C\limits_\alpha {H_2}}\limits_{Alkyl\;chloride} - Cl + KOH(alc) \to{/tex}{tex}\mathop {R - CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{Alkene} + KCl + {H_2}O{/tex}
{tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion is a much weaker base than {tex} RO^ -{/tex} ion. Also, {tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion is highly solvated in an aqueous solution and as a result, the basic character of {tex}O{H^\;}^{ - \;}{/tex} ion decreases. Therefore, it cannot abstract a hydrogen from the {tex}\beta {/tex}-carbon.{tex}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Primary alkyl halide C 4 H 9 Br (a) reacted with alcoholic KOH to give compound (b).Compound (b) is reacted with HBr to give (c) which is an isomer of (a). When (a) is reacted with sodium metal it gives compound (d), {tex}{C_8}{H_{18}}{/tex} which is different from the compound formed when n-butyl bromide is reacted with sodium. Give the structural formula of (a) and write the equations for all the reactions.
question_html: Primary alkyl halide C4H9Br (a) reacted with alcoholic KOH to give compound (b).Compound (b) is reacted with HBr to give (c) which is an isomer of (a). When (a) is reacted with sodium metal it gives compound (d), {tex}{C_8}{H_{18}}{/tex} which is different from the compound formed when n-butyl bromide is reacted with sodium. Give the structural formula of (a) and write the equations for all the reactions.
solution_text: Two primary alkyl halides with molecular formula C 4 H 9 Br are possible. They are n-butyl bromide and isobutyl bromide. When (a) is reacted with sodium metal it gives compound (d), C 8 H 18 which is different from the compound formed when n-butyl bromide is reacted with sodium. Hence, compound a is isobutyl bromide and compound d is 2, 5-dimethylhexane. 2CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 Br + 2Na {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Wurtz}\;\mathrm{reaction}}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 (n-octane) 2CH 3 CH(CH 3 )CH 2 Br + 2Na {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{wurtz}\;\mathrm{reaction}}{/tex} CH 3 CH(CH 3 )CH 2 CH 2 CH(CH 3 )CH 3 (2, 5-dimethylhexane) (a) reacted with alcoholic KOH to give compound (b). CH 3 CH(CH 3 )CH 2 Br {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{alcKOH}}{/tex} CH 3 C(CH 3 ) = CH 2 (2 - methyl - 1 - propene) Compound (b) is reacted with HBr to give (c) which is an isomer of (a). CH 3 - C(CH 3 ) = CH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{Markownikoff}'\mathrm s\;\mathrm{rule}}]{\mathrm{HBr}}{/tex} CH 3 - CBr(CH 3 ) CH 3 (tert - butylbromide)
solution_html:
Two primary alkyl halides with molecular formula C4H9Br are possible. They are n-butyl bromide and isobutyl bromide.
When (a) is reacted with sodium metal it gives compound (d), C8H18 which is different from the compound formed when n-butyl bromide is reacted with sodium. Hence, compound a is isobutyl bromide and compound d is 2, 5-dimethylhexane.
2CH3CH2CH2CH2Br + 2Na {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Wurtz}\;\mathrm{reaction}}{/tex} CH3CH2CH2CH2CH2CH2CH2CH3 (n-octane)
2CH3CH(CH3)CH2Br + 2Na {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{wurtz}\;\mathrm{reaction}}{/tex} CH3CH(CH3)CH2CH2CH(CH3)CH3(2, 5-dimethylhexane)
(a) reacted with alcoholic KOH to give compound (b).
CH3CH(CH3)CH2Br {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{alcKOH}}{/tex} CH3C(CH3) = CH2(2 - methyl - 1 - propene)
Compound (b) is reacted with HBr to give (c) which is an isomer of (a).
CH3 - C(CH3) = CH2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{Markownikoff}'\mathrm s\;\mathrm{rule}}]{\mathrm{HBr}}{/tex} CH3 - CBr(CH3) CH3 (tert - butylbromide)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What happens when n-butyl chloride is treated with alcoholic KOH, bromobenzene is treated with Mg in the presence of dry ether, chlorobenzene is subjected to hydrolysis, ethyl chloride is treated with aqueous KOH, methyl bromide is treated with sodium in the presence of dry ether, methyl chloride is treated with KCN.
question_html:
What happens when

  1. n-butyl chloride is treated with alcoholic KOH,
  2. bromobenzene is treated with Mg in the presence of dry ether,
  3. chlorobenzene is subjected to hydrolysis,
  4. ethyl chloride is treated with aqueous KOH,
  5. methyl bromide is treated with sodium in the presence of dry ether,
  6. methyl chloride is treated with KCN.
solution_text: When n - butyl chloride is treated with alcoholic KOH, the formation of but - 1 - ene takes place. This reaction is a dehydrohalogenation reaction. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{n }} - {\text{ Butyl chloride}}} - C{H_2} - Cl{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{{\text{(dehydrohalogenation}})}]{{KOH(alc)/\Delta }}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{But - 1 - ene} + KCl + {H_2}O{/tex} When bromobenzene is treated with Mg in the presence of dry ether, phenylmagnesium bromide is formed. Chlorobenzene does not undergo hydrolysis under normal conditions. However, it undergoes hydrolysis when heated in an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution at a temperature of 623 K and a pressure of 300 atm to form phenol. When ethyl chloride is treated with aqueous KOH, it undergoes hydrolysis to form ethanol. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - Cl}\limits_{Ethyl\;chloride} \xrightarrow[{(Hydrolysis)}]{{KO{H_{(aq)}}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2}}\limits_{Ethanol} - OH + KCl{/tex} When methyl bromide is treated with sodium in the presence of dry ether, ethane is formed. This reaction is known as the Wurtz reaction. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - Br + 2Na}\limits_{Methyl\;bromide} \xrightarrow[{(wurtz\;reaction)}]{{Dry\;ether}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethane} + 2NaBr{/tex} When methyl chloride is treated with KCN, it undergoes a substitution reaction to give methyl cyanide. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - Cl}\limits_{Methyl\;chloride} + KCN{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{{\text{nucleophilic substitution}}}]{}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - CN}\limits_{Methyl\;cyanide} + KCl{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When n - butyl chloride is treated with alcoholic KOH, the formation of but - 1 - ene takes place. This reaction is a dehydrohalogenation reaction.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{n }} - {\text{ Butyl chloride}}} - C{H_2} - Cl{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{{\text{(dehydrohalogenation}})}]{{KOH(alc)/\Delta }}{/tex}{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = C{H_2}}\limits_{But - 1 - ene} + KCl + {H_2}O{/tex}
  2. When bromobenzene is treated with Mg in the presence of dry ether, phenylmagnesium bromide is formed.
  3. Chlorobenzene does not undergo hydrolysis under normal conditions. However, it undergoes hydrolysis when heated in an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution at a temperature of 623 K and a pressure of 300 atm to form phenol.
  4. When ethyl chloride is treated with aqueous KOH, it undergoes hydrolysis to form ethanol.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - Cl}\limits_{Ethyl\;chloride} \xrightarrow[{(Hydrolysis)}]{{KO{H_{(aq)}}}}{/tex}{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2}}\limits_{Ethanol} - OH + KCl{/tex}
  5. When methyl bromide is treated with sodium in the presence of dry ether, ethane is formed. This reaction is known as the Wurtz reaction.
    {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - Br + 2Na}\limits_{Methyl\;bromide} \xrightarrow[{(wurtz\;reaction)}]{{Dry\;ether}}{/tex}{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethane} + 2NaBr{/tex}
  6. When methyl chloride is treated with KCN, it undergoes a substitution reaction to give methyl cyanide.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - Cl}\limits_{Methyl\;chloride} + KCN{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{{\text{nucleophilic substitution}}}]{}{/tex}{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - CN}\limits_{Methyl\;cyanide} + KCl{/tex}
